title: End of module: Stationary Random Processes

questions.text: A random process X(t) is best described as:
choices: A family of random variables, one for every time t
A single fixed function of time with no randomness
A random variable that only takes one value regardless of t
questions.explanation: A random process assigns a random variable to every instant t; a single realization (one recording) is just one outcome drawn simultaneously across all those instants.

questions.text: Across five realizations, at a fixed time t the readings are 2, 2, 4, 4, 3. What is the ensemble average E[X(t)] at that instant?
A process is wide-sense stationary (WSS) when:
choices: Its mean function is constant, and its autocorrelation depends only on the lag τ = t₁ − t₂
Every joint distribution of any collection of samples is exactly shift-invariant
It has zero variance at every time
questions.explanation: WSS is the weaker second-order condition: constant mean and lag-only autocorrelation. Full shift-invariance of every joint distribution is the stronger condition, strict-sense stationarity (SSS).

choices: No — WSS only controls first and second moments; higher-order structure could still change over time
Yes — WSS and SSS are always equivalent
Yes, but only for discrete-time processes
questions.explanation: WSS is a strictly weaker condition than SSS in general. The one major exception is a jointly Gaussian process, whose distribution is fully determined by its mean and covariance alone.

questions.text: For a jointly Gaussian process, why does WSS imply SSS?
choices: A Gaussian joint distribution is fully determined by its mean and covariance, so shift-invariance of those (WSS) forces shift-invariance of the entire distribution
Gaussian processes have no autocorrelation function
SSS and WSS are unrelated concepts for Gaussian processes specifically
questions.explanation: A Gaussian distribution carries no information beyond its mean and covariance, so if those are already shift-invariant (WSS), the entire distribution built from them must be too (SSS).

choices: A single realization's time average converges to the true ensemble average
Every realization of the process is identical
The process's mean must be exactly zero
questions.explanation: A process is (mean-)ergodic when the time average computed from one sufficiently long realization converges to the ensemble mean — the property that justifies estimating ensemble statistics from a single recording.

questions.text: Why is X(t) = A (A drawn once per realization, then frozen for all t) a WSS process that fails to be ergodic?
choices: A single realization never explores A's other possible values, so its time average only reflects the one value A happened to take, not the true ensemble average
Because X(t) = A isn't actually a valid random process
Because its autocorrelation function isn't symmetric
questions.explanation: Within one realization, A is fixed forever — a time average of that realization just reports A's frozen value, never converging to the true ensemble mean E[A], since it never samples A's other possible outcomes.

questions.text: A zero-mean WSS process has R_X(0) = 12 and R_X(3) = 15. Since |R_X(τ)| ≤ R_X(0) must hold for a genuine autocorrelation function, is this candidate valid? Enter 1 for valid, 0 for invalid.
questions.explanation: R_X(3) = 15 exceeds R_X(0) = 12, violating |R_X(τ)| ≤ R_X(0) — this candidate cannot be a genuine autocorrelation function.

questions.text: One word: for a real WSS process, R_X(τ) is always symmetric about τ = 0 — mathematicians call a function with this property ___.
questions.explanation: R_X(−τ) = R_X(τ) for any real WSS process is exactly the definition of an even function.

questions.text: Cumulative review: from probability-foundations, Cov(X,X) equals Var(X). How does this relate to R_X(0) for a zero-mean WSS process?
choices: R_X(0) = E[X(t)²] = Var(X) for zero-mean X, exactly the Cov(X,X) = Var(X) fact applied at zero lag
They are unrelated quantities computed from different formulas
R_X(0) is always zero regardless of Var(X)
questions.explanation: From probability-foundations: Cov(X,X) = Var(X). R_X(0) = E[X(t)X(t)] = E[X(t)²], which for a zero-mean process equals Var(X) directly — the same identity, just applied to a signal's autocorrelation at zero lag.
